title: Stop explaining black box machine learning models for high stakes decisions and use interpretable models instead

data_scope.paper_type: Perspective / commentary — no primary data collection
data_scope.recidivism_data: Broward County FL defendant records (ProPublica COMPAS); exact n not stated in article
data_scope.credit_data: FICO 2018 challenge dataset; size not stated
data_scope.image_data: CUB-200 ~12,000 images, 200 bird species (referenced via prototype network papers)
data_scope.nyc_grid_data: Multi-source infrastructure records 1890s-present; no volume stated
data_scope.data_availability: No original data released; CORELS and RiskSLIM publicly available separately

core_conclusion: Post hoc explainability of black box models is not a substitute for inherent interpretability: explanations are always unfaithful to the original model, often misleading, and can perpetuate serious harm in criminal justice, healthcare and other high-stakes domains. The accuracy-interpretability trade-off is a myth for structured data — interpretable models (rule lists, scoring systems, prototype networks) routinely match black box accuracy. Society should demand inherently interpretable models, not post hoc explanations, for high-stakes decisions.
why_it_matters: Reframes the dominant XAI paradigm by arguing post hoc explanation is solving the wrong problem; documents real-world harms of COMPAS (130+ factors, no accuracy advantage over a 3-rule model, typographical-error unfairness); establishes the Rashomon-set theoretical guarantee that interpretable accuracy is achievable; proposes concrete governance mandates auditable under GDPR-style regulation.
why_nature: Paradigm-challenging perspective piece with broad societal relevance across criminal justice, healthcare and environmental safety; cross-disciplinary scope spanning CS, criminology, statistics and public policy; directly actionable for AI regulators.
added_value_to_my_research: Rashomon-set audit: test whether sparse MGWR/GWR matches GBM accuracy for spatial prediction before accepting black-box GeoAI models
RiskSLIM integer scoring systems as interpretable alternatives to SHAP-attributed XGBoost for healthcare accessibility prediction
Spatial equity extension of CORELS: add neighborhood-level features to recidivism-style models to audit geographic clustering of decision errors
Prototype network architecture appended to geospatial foundation models (SatMAE, DOFA) for interpretable land-use classification
Interpretability compliance layer in spatiotemporal public-health ML pipelines: formal audit proving no interpretable baseline matches black-box accuracy before deployment

related.title: Multiple stakeholders drive diverse interpretability requirements for machine learning in healthcare
related.relation: extends Rudin's interpretability thesis to domain-specific stakeholder needs in healthcare ML

related.title: Algorithmic fairness and bias mitigation for clinical machine learning with deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: addresses the fairness/bias dimension of black-box clinical models that Rudin identifies as a risk

related.title: On responsible machine learning datasets emphasizing fairness, privacy and regulatory norms with examples in biometrics and healthcare
related.relation: operationalizes the responsible-AI governance principles Rudin advocates

related.title: Learning with explainable trees
related.relation: proposes tree-based methods as inherently interpretable alternatives — directly aligned with Rudin's Challenge 1

related.title: Morphological and molecular breast cancer profiling through explainable machine learning
related.relation: healthcare application demonstrating the tension between black-box accuracy and interpretability that Rudin critiques

figures.caption: A fictional depiction of the accuracy-interpretability trade-off (adapted from DARPA XAI BAA) — used to debunk the myth that complexity is required for accuracy
Saliency maps for dog vs. musical instrument look essentially identical — demonstrating that post hoc visual explanations provide no mechanistic understanding
Prototype network (This Looks Like That) for bird classification — each prediction is an inherently interpretable comparison to learned prototypical image patches
